pattern: Each cell's value defines a whole reachable span along its row and column at once, so the shortest path to the far corner is a breadth-first search over spans rather than single-step neighbors.
hints: Model each cell as a node whose outgoing edges are every cell reachable in its row up to the cell's value, plus every cell reachable in its column up to that same value.

Explore the grid breadth-first from the top-left cell, expanding each frontier cell across its whole allowed row range and column range in one go, and stop as soon as the bottom-right cell is first reached.
Track a visited/distance grid so no cell is expanded twice; the answer is the distance recorded for the bottom-right cell (counting the starting cell itself), or -1 if it is never reached.

edge_inputs.description: a 4x4 grid with a path shorter than the naive corner-hugging route
a 4x4 grid where the shortest path is only three cells
a grid where every path stalls before reaching the last cell
a single cell grid, start equals destination
the starting cell has value zero but is not the destination, so no move is possible
a single row grid solvable by one big rightward jump
a single column grid solvable by one big downward jump
a grid where the direct row route is blocked but a detour through a column reaches the corner

stress.description: random grid up to 20x20 whose values are small jump ranges, so both reachable and unreachable corners occur across seeds
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 20))
    n = max(1, min(size, 20))
    grid = [[rng.randrange(0, 5) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    grid[m - 1][n - 1] = 0
    return [grid]
stress.note: small jump ranges (0-4) on a grid up to 20x20 keep the O((m*n)^2)-ish naive BFS well under the time budget
